overview: This week I moved my RAG pipeline from a local ChromaDB prototype to a production-ready system backed by Pinecone, and added agentic retrieval using an MCP-pattern tool server. I also rebuilt my Personalization Strategy Assistant v3 with real Pinecone data, multi-agent architecture, and live ROI calculations — the most complex thing I've deployed so far.

keyPoints.title: ChromaDB replaced by Pinecone to solve four production-blocking failures
keyPoints.description: ChromaDB broke down on Streamlit Cloud due to no persistence, single-process writes, no backups, and slow metadata filtering above ~10K docs — all solved by migrating to Pinecone in 30-45 minutes.

keyPoints.title: Agentic RAG scored 0.3 points higher but cost 66% more tokens on average
keyPoints.description: My head-to-head LLM-judge comparison showed agentic RAG averaged 4.3/5 vs. standard RAG's 4.0/5, but the complex strategic question — where multi-search should shine most — was a tie despite 5 searches and +188% token overhead.

keyPoints.title: MCP pattern refactored tools into a single importable module for all agents
keyPoints.description: I consolidated three tools — Pinecone search, temporal context, and ROI calculator — into one file with a shared tool menu and dispatcher, so any new tool I add is automatically available to every agent that imports the module.

keyPoints.title: Strategy Assistant v3 is my most complex deployment: three workers, real data, live ROI
keyPoints.description: I rebuilt the assistant with a Planner → Workers → Synthesizer architecture where three independent worker agents run in sequence, each making their own tool use decisions against real Pinecone data and a live ROI calculator.

keyPoints.title: Standard vs. agentic RAG is a product tradeoff, not a clear upgrade
keyPoints.description: The quality edge of agentic RAG is real but small, and the cost difference only makes sense for genuinely complex queries — simpler use cases don't justify the token overhead.
keyPoints.sectionName: Standard vs. Agentic RAG Is a Product Decision

keyPoints.title: Pinecone's idempotent upsert prevented duplicate vectors during migration
keyPoints.description: Because Pinecone treats same-ID upserts as updates rather than inserts, I could safely re-run ingestion without polluting the index — a detail that matters far more in production than in local prototyping.
keyPoints.sectionName: Idempotent Operations Matter More Than You'd Think
